coredata.dc:title: Unveiling lithium storage potential in Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes: A study of structural stability, electronic properties, and adsorption behavior
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Teleb, Nahed H.
Abdelrazek, Ghada M.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates lithium (Li) adsorption on Zr2C and oxygen-passivated Zr2CO2 MXenes using density functional theory (DFT) to assess their potential for energy storage. The results show Zr2C, with a binding energy of −8.09 eV, to be more stable than Zr2CO2 (−6.88 eV). Analysis of partial density of states (PDOS) and HOMO-LUMO gap indicates that oxygen passivation broadens the energy gap from 0.78 eV (Zr2C) to 1.38 eV (Zr2CO2), improving electronic stability. Both materials exhibit strong Li adsorption with an energy of −3.35 eV, suggesting favorable adsorption. Furthermore, uniform adsorption energies across surface and edge sites allow flexible Li storage. Zr2C’s high charge transfer and favorable interactions result in an impressive theoretical capacity of 2894.47 mAhg−1, significantly surpassing graphite’s 37 mAhg−1. These findings position Zr2C as a promising candidate for stable, high-capacity lithium-ion battery electrodes, with notable advantages over conventional graphite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zr2C MXene
Zr2CO2
Density functional theory (DFT)
Lithium-ion batteries
Adsorption energy
Energy storage materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption of one Li atom on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 mxenes
PDOS and HOMO/LUMO representation of Zr2C/Zr2CO2-Li
Average open circuit voltage and storage capacity of Zr2C

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, and b) Front and side views of Zr2 C, and Zr2 CO2 MXenes.
(a-b) HOMO/LUMO representations, HOMO energy (EH), LUMO energy (EL), and energy gap (Eg) of Zr2 C and Zr2 CO2 .

(a, and f) the sites at which the adsorption of Li atom is done for Zr2 C and Zr2 CO2 . (b-e, and g-j) the optimized structures for Zr2 C, and Zr2 CO2 MXenes after the adsorption of one Li atom at different sites.

(a-e) Calculated PDOS, HOMO energy (EH), LUMO energy (EL), and energy gap (Eg) for Zr2 C before and after the adsorption of one Li atom at different sites (A-D). The Y-axis represents the density of states in units of eV−1 cm−2 , while the X-axis shows energy in eV. The dotted vertical line indicates the Fermi level. The HOMO lies to the right of the Fermi level, while the LUMO is located to the left of the Fermi level. The barcodes at the bottom are a way of visually representing which orbitals (and their energies) are contributing to the PDOS at any given point.

(a-e) Calculated PDOS, EH , EL , and Eg for Zr2 CO2 before and after the adsorption of one Li atom at different sites (A-D). The Y-axis represents the density of states in units of eV−1 cm−2 , while the X-axis shows energy in eV. The dotted vertical line indicates the Fermi level. The HOMO lies to the right of the Fermi level, while the LUMO is located to the left of the Fermi level. The barcodes at the bottom are a way of visually representing which orbitals (and their energies) are contributing to the PDOS at any given point.
(a-d) Optimized structures of Zr2C with varying lithium concentrations: Zr2C-3Li, Zr2C-10Li, Zr2C-15Li, and Zr2C-21Li.
Adsorption energy (Ea) of one Li atom for Zr2 C, and Zr2 CO2 MXenes at different sites (A-D). Also, the Mulliken charge (MC) of the Li atom is calculated after the adsorption process.
Adsorption energy (Ea) and average open circuit voltage (Vave) of Li atoms for Zr2 C MXene.

head.ce:title: Unveiling lithium storage potential in Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes: A study of structural stability, electronic properties, and adsorption behavior

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates lithium (Li) adsorption on Zr2C and oxygen-passivated Zr2CO2 MXenes using density functional theory (DFT) to assess their potential for energy storage. The results show Zr2C, with a binding energy of −8.09 eV, to be more stable than Zr2CO2 (−6.88 eV). Analysis of partial density of states (PDOS) and HOMO-LUMO gap indicates that oxygen passivation broadens the energy gap from 0.78 eV (Zr2C) to 1.38 eV (Zr2CO2), improving electronic stability. Both materials exhibit strong Li adsorption with an energy of −3.35 eV, suggesting favorable adsorption. Furthermore, uniform adsorption energies across surface and edge sites allow flexible Li storage. Zr2C’s high charge transfer and favorable interactions result in an impressive theoretical capacity of 2894.47 mAhg−1, significantly surpassing graphite’s 37 mAhg−1. These findings position Zr2C as a promising candidate for stable, high-capacity lithium-ion battery electrodes, with notable advantages over conventional graphite.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Portable devices, electric vehicles, and smart electronics increasingly depend on rechargeable batteries with high power density, energy density, and extended cycle life to replace traditional energy sources like solar, biomass, and wind [1–5]. The performance of these devices is closely tied to the anode, which plays a crucial role in metal-ion battery functionality [6]. While graphite is a widely used anode material known for its cyclic stability, affordability, and high coulombic efficiency, its specific capacity remains limited to 372 mAh g−1, alongside challenges like low ion diffusivity, high irreversible capacity, and volume expansion [7,8]. Therefore, advanced materials offering greater storage capacity and fewer limitations are essential to meet future energy demands. Recently, two-dimensional (2D) materials [9–13], known for their high surface-to-volume ratio and unique electronic, magnetic, and optical properties, have shown promise in energy storage applications [14,15]. Widely used rechargeable ion batteries, including lithium-ion (LIBs) [16], sodium-ion (NaIBs) [17], potassium-ion (KIBs) [18,19], magnesium-ion (MgIBs) [20], and sulfur-ion (SIBs) [21], encounter challenges such as low capacity, slow charge/discharge rates, and poor cycle stability [22,23]. Overcoming these limitations is critical for their viability in future applications, and single-layer 2D materials offer potential solutions due to their advantageous electrochemical properties [24,25]. Graphene, for instance, has been extensively studied as an anode material, exhibiting high reversible capacity, fast rate capability, and long cycle life [26–28].

In recent studies, Benzidi et al. investigated the adsorption of alkali metals (Li, Na) on monolayer arsenene, reporting high storage capacities (1609 mAh g−1 and 1073 mAh g−1) with low diffusion barriers (161 meV and 51 meV) and open-circuit voltages of 0.55/0.29 V for Li-/Na-ion batteries [29]. Mortazavi et al. examined the interaction of Li and Na with borophene, identifying high storage capacities of 1380 mAh g−1 and 1720 mAh g−1, respectively, with diffusion barriers ranging from 25–317 meV for Li and 3–223 meV for Na in various directions [30]. Jiang et al. demonstrated boron phosphide’s (BP) potential as an anode material for Li-, Na-, and K-ion batteries with capacities of 1283 mAh g−1 for Li and 570 mAh g−1 for K, alongside low energy barriers of 0.3 eV, 0.2 eV, and 0.15 eV for Li, Na, and K ions, respectively [31]. Vadym et al. studied Na adsorption on phosphorene monolayers, noting a capacity of 865 mAh g−1 and a low diffusion barrier of 0.04 eV, highlighting its potential as a NIB anode [32]. To advance rechargeable battery technologies, it is vital to develop anode materials that achieve high diffusion rates, greater cycle durability, and enhanced storage capacity [33].

Zirconium-based MXenes have gained significant attention due to their unique electronic, structural, and electrochemical properties. Previous studies have explored various aspects of these materials. For instance, García-Romeral et al. investigated the electronic ground states of M2C MXenes (M = Ti, V, Cr, Zr, Nb, Mo, Hf, Ta, and W), highlighting their diverse electronic properties and potential applications [34]. Wang et al. examined the electronic contacts and lubricity characteristics of Zr2C and Zr2CY2 (Y = F or OH) when interfaced with WSe2, providing insights into their interfacial behavior and possible applications in tribology and electronics [35]. Additionally, Wang et al. demonstrated the potential of Zr2C as an anode material for Li, Na, and K-ion batteries, emphasizing its high capacity and stable performance under varying conditions [36].

Bismuthene, a newly developed 2D material comprising bismuth atoms in a hexagonal honeycomb structure, exhibits favorable properties for energy storage. Its spin–orbit coupling (SOC) characteristics are crucial for accurate lattice constant, band gap, and formation energy determination [37]. β-phase bismuthene (β-Bi), a non-magnetic semiconductor, shows strong stability across thermal, excitational, and vibrational conditions, making it suitable for spintronic devices, data transmission, and electrochemical applications [38]. Additionally, β-Bi’s narrow band gap, high thermal stability, and mechanical strength support its use in alkali metal adsorption, positioning it as a promising electrode material for ion batteries, potentially surpassing other 2D materials in practical applications. Since graphene’s discovery, the unique properties and applications of 2D layered materials have spurred tremendous interest. Graphene, B4C3, siliborophene, phosphorene, and dichalcogenides (e.g., NbSe2, MoS2) have been extensively studied as electrode materials for LIBs [39–48], and MoS2/graphene 2D composites have shown promise as NIB anodes [49]. A newer family of 2D materials, MXenes (transition metal carbides and carbonitrides, Mn+1Xn, where n = 1, 2, 3 and X = C or N), has gained significant attention. Derived from MAX phase precursors (A is a group element like Al), these materials include over 60 phases, with Ti3C2 being the first MXene synthesized by etching Ti3AlC2 with hydrofluoric acid (HF) [50–53]. Since then, MXenes like Ti3CN, Ti2C, (V0.5Cr0.5)3C2, (Ti0.5Nb0.5)2C, Nb2C, V2C, and Ta4C3 have been successfully developed [50–55]. In this study, we explored the adsorption characteristics of a single lithium (Li) atom on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes using density functional theory (DFT) to understand their potential for energy storage applications. The results show that Zr2C exhibits higher stability, with a binding energy of 8.099 eV, compared to 6.883 eV for Zr2CO2. Zr2C was chosen as the representative material for evaluating average open-circuit voltage and storage capacity due to its favorable balance of stability and performance. These findings highlight the potential of Zr2C for future lithium-ion battery applications, while also revealing the advantages of surface modifications for optimizing MXene materials.

In this study, we performed density functional theory (DFT) [56]simulations on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes using Gaussian 16 software [57] to investigate their optimized structures, electronic properties, and potential as lithium storage materials [58]. The hybrid M06 functional [12,59], known for its reliability in providing accurate insights into optimized geometries and electronic properties [60–63], was employed alongside the LANL2DZ basis set [64], which offers a balance between computational efficiency and precision [65,66]. To accurately capture the Van der Waals interactions essential for describing the interactions between Zr2​C, Zr2​CO2​, and a lithium atom, we included Grimme's dispersion correction (gd3) with the M06 functional [67]. We further analyzed the electronic structure using Multiwfn to calculate the partial density of states (PDOS) [68] and examined the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), and Mulliken charge distributions [69]. Due to the high computational requirements, adsorption studies involving multiple lithium atoms and Zr2C MXenes were performed using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [70,71]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was applied to model electron exchange and correlation [72]. A plane-wave kinetic energy cutoff of 520 eV was applied, with convergence criteria set at 10−5 eV for energy and −0.01 eV/Å for atomic forces. Additionally, Van der Waals interactions were accounted in VASP calculations using the DFT-D3 method of Grimme et al. [67]. Since VASP calculations are performed on finite MXene quantum dots, sufficient vacuum space is added in all directions (a = b = 20 Å and c = 14 Å) around the nanodots to prevent any possible interactions between the nanodot and its periodic images.

Fig. 1 (a and b) presents the front and side views of Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes, with atomic symbols numbered in the side views to provide clarity on structural parameters such as bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles. Both structures have square shapes, with a total of 45 and 73 atoms for the Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes, respectively. The side views also highlight the oxygen passivation on the above and below surfaces of Zr2C. For Zr2C, the Zr1-C1 and Zr1-C2 bond lengths are 2.137 Å and 2.228 Å, respectively. The C2-Zr1-C1 bond angle is 83.427 degrees, and the Zr3-C2-Zr1-C1 dihedral angle is 179.414 degrees. In Zr2CO2, the Zr1-C1 and Zr1-C2 bond lengths increase to 2.310 Å and 2.499 Å, respectively. The C2-Zr1-C1 bond angle increases slightly to 83.565 degrees, while the Zr3-C2-Zr1-C1 dihedral angle decreases to 176.985 degrees. These results show that oxygen passivation increases the Zr1-C1 and Zr1-C2 bond lengths, as well as the C2-Zr1-C1 bond angle while decreasing the Zr3-C2-Zr1-C1 dihedral angle. The change in the dihedral angle affects the planarity of the square shape in the side view, with Zr2CO2 exhibiting a lower pronounced deviation from planarity compared to Zr2C. The side views also illustrate that Zr2C consists of a single square shape, whereas Zr2CO2 is composed of two to three square shapes, indicating a thicker structure due to oxygen passivation on the above and below surfaces. These structural changes due to oxygen passivation could significantly impact the material's properties and potential applications.

The stability of Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes was investigated by calculating their binding energy (BE). The BE was determined using the following formula: BE= (Et-(EZr × NZr + EC × NC + EO × NO))/ Nt, where EZr, EC, EO, and Et represent the energy of Zr, C, O, and the total energy of the studied compounds, respectively. NZr, NC, NO, and Nt, denote the number of Zr, C, O, and the total number of atoms in the studied structure, respectively. The calculated BE values for Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes are −8.099 eV and −6.883 eV, respectively. These negative BE values indicate the energetic stability of the studied compounds. The higher BE of Zr2C suggests that it has greater stability compared to Zr2CO2. This implies that passivating Zr2C with O atoms on both the top and bottom surfaces to form Zr2CO2 enhances the reactivity of the material. In practical applications, the increased reactivity of Zr2CO2 can be advantageous for catalytic processes, energy storage, and sensor technologies. The modification of Zr2C by oxygen passivation not only tailors its electronic properties but also potentially enhances its performance in various chemical reactions. These findings underscore the importance of surface modification in tuning the properties of MXenes for specific applications, paving the way for the development of highly efficient and stable materials in the fields of catalysis, energy storage, and beyond. For comparison, Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2 have a BE of −7.335, −8.154, and −6.704 eV [13], suggesting that Zr2C and Zr2CO2 are more stable. The higher BE of Zr2C shows it is more stable than Zr2CO2, where oxygen passivation enhances reactivity, useful for catalysis and energy storage.

Fig. 2 (a-b) illustrates the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) representations for Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes. In these graphs, the HOMO and LUMO in both Zr2C and Zr2CO2 are predominantly localized on specific Zr and C atoms, with additional contributions from O atoms in Zr2CO2. This localization is primarily observed on the Zr-C and Zr-O bonds, which provides insights into the electronic stability and structural integrity of these MXenes. The strong covalent interactions within the Zr-C bonds in Zr2C contribute significantly to its electronic stability. In Zr2CO2, the Zr-O bonds introduce enhanced bond polarity, leading to a modified electronic structure that improves the material's reactivity while maintaining overall stability. These localized electronic features confirm that the HOMO and LUMO play a key role in determining the stability and performance of these MXenes, making them promising candidates for applications requiring robust electronic and structural properties. The energy gap (Eg) is calculated using the equation: Eg = EH − EL, where EH and EL are the energies of the HOMO and LUMO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 (a-b), the oxygen passivation on both the upper and lower surfaces of Zr2C to form Zr2CO2 significantly affects the orbital energies. The HOMO energy decreases from −3.53 eV in Zr2C to −4.60 eV in Zr2CO2. Similarly, the LUMO energy decreases from −2.75 eV in Zr2C to −3.21 eV in Zr2CO2. As a result, the energy gap increases from 0.78 eV in Zr2C to 1.38 eV in Zr2CO2 due to the passivation by O atoms. This increase in the energy gap suggests that the passivation of Zr2C with O atoms modifies its electronic properties, making Zr2CO2 more suitable for applications in energy storage, particularly in lithium-ion batteries. The larger energy gap can contribute to improved performance and efficiency in these applications. Furthermore, the localization of HOMO and LUMO on the Zr-C and Zr-O bonds indicates robust bonding interactions, which are crucial for the stability and performance of materials used in energy storage systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of one Li atom on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 mxenes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a, f) illustrates the potential adsorption sites (A, B, C, D) for a lithium (Li) atom on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes. Sites A and B are positioned above edge C and Zr atoms, while sites C and D are above surface C and Zr atoms. Table 1 presents the adsorption energy (Ea) and Mulliken charge (MC) of a Li atom at these sites. A more negative Ea indicates a stronger Li-MXene bond and negative values signify spontaneous adsorption. Analyzing the Ea values, the surface sites (C, D) on Zr2C are more favorable for Li storage compared to the edge sites (A, B) due to stronger adsorption, with the most negative Ea observed at site D (−3.354 eV). For Zr2CO2, the adsorption energies are relatively similar across all sites, with site A (surface) showing the strongest binding energy (−3.546 eV). This trend suggests that surface sites are generally more favorable for Li adsorption. Additionally, the MC values provide insight into the charge transfer between Li atoms and MXenes during the adsorption process. Before adsorption, the Li atom has a charge of zero. Post-adsorption, the MC values for Zr2C indicate that the Li atoms at sites C and D exhibit significant charge transfer, with MC values of 0.532 and 0.495, respectively. This substantial charge transfer suggests strong interactions between the Li atoms and the MXene surface atoms. The edge sites (A and B) show lower MC values of 0.058 and 0.346, indicating weaker interactions. This difference in charge transfer between the edge sites and the surface sites of Zr2C suggests that the adsorption at the central sites is more favorable, likely contributing to the higher overall adsorption energy observed at these locations. The validity of our models, in terms of charge distribution and valence states, has been carefully considered. Zr atoms are assumed to be in their + 4 oxidation state, consistent with their position in the periodic table, while C is treated as in its typical −4 state. This ensures proper valence and avoids any unphysical bonding or charge imbalances. For Zr2CO2, the MC values are somewhat uniform across the sites, with site D showing the highest MC (0.465), indicating that Li atoms adsorbed at this site experience significant charge transfer. The similarity in MC values across sites for Zr2CO2 (ranging from 0.307 to 0.465) aligns with the relatively uniform adsorption energies, further suggesting that the electronic environment of Zr2CO2 is more homogeneous compared to Zr2C. In this model, the valence of Zr atoms has been maintained at + 4, and the O atoms in CO2 are assumed to be in their −2 oxidation state, which aligns with typical bonding patterns. The uniform charge transfer across the sites indicates a more homogeneous electronic structure, where charge compensation is effectively managed across the surface. The data implies that Zr2C is a superior material for Li storage due to the highly negative adsorption energy and significant charge transfer at surface sites. The stronger charge transfer observed at surface sites also indicates that Zr2C may have a higher potential for lithium storage capacity and better cycling stability compared to Zr2CO2, where the interactions appear to be more uniform but weaker overall. The strong binding affinity and charge interaction at surface sites underscore the potential of Zr2C for efficient Li storage applications.

Our results align with and expand upon the findings of previous studies on Zr-based MXenes. For example, García-Romeral et al. reported the metallic nature of Zr2C, consistent with our observation of its high density of states at the Fermi level, which facilitates excellent electronic conductivity [34]. Similarly, our findings support the conclusions of Wang et al., who noted the strong adsorption capabilities of Zr2C for alkali metal ions, making it a promising anode material [36]. In the case of Zr2CO2, we observed enhanced interaction with Li due to the presence of surface O atoms, leading to higher adsorption energies compared to bare Zr2C. This aligns with Wang et al.’s discussion of functionalized Zr-based MXenes, where surface termination significantly alters their electrochemical properties [35]. Our study provides additional quantitative insights into the specific role of surface functionalities in enhancing Li-ion storage capacity. Moreover, while Wang et al. focused on Li, Na, and K adsorption on Zr2C, our work extends this understanding by providing a comparative analysis of the electronic properties and adsorption energies for pristine and oxygen-terminated Zr-based MXenes, emphasizing their potential for high-performance Li-ion battery applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PDOS and HOMO/LUMO representation of Zr2C/Zr2CO2-Li
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 (a-h) shows the HOMO and LUMO representations for Zr2C (a-d) and Zr2CO2 (e-h) after Li atom adsorption at various sites (A-D). In Zr2C, the orbitals localize on Zr and C atoms, while in Zr2CO2, they localize on Zr, C, and O atoms. The Li atom contributes to LUMO localization in Zr2CO2-A-Li and Zr2CO2-B-Li but not at other sites. These results indicate that oxygen in Zr2CO2 influences electronic distribution, allowing Li participation in LUMO localization. This site-specific interaction suggests that the electronic properties of Zr-based compounds can be tailored through Li adsorption, especially in the presence of oxygen.

Fig. 4 (a-e) and 5 (a-e) illustrate the partial density of states (PDOS) for Zr2C and Zr2CO2, respectively, before and after the adsorption of a single Li atom at various sites (A-D). The broadening factor (FWHM) used in the PDOS calculations is set to 0.1 eV to accurately present the gap region and the PDOS peaks. Both figures present the HOMO energy (EH), LUMO energy (EL), and energy gap (Eg) of Zr2C and Zr2CO2 before and after Li adsorption at different sites. As observed in Fig. 4 (a-e), the LUMO PDOS of Zr in Zr2C is significantly higher than that of C, indicating that the Zr atom acts as an electron acceptor. Conversely, the HOMO PDOS for C is higher than that for Zr, suggesting that the C atom serves as an electron donor. Upon Li adsorption at various sites (A–D), the LUMO PDOS peak for Zr exhibits slight broadening compared to its state before adsorption. This is confirmed by calculating the bandwidth (B.W.) for Zr2C and Zr2C-A-Li as representative cases, with B.W. values of 1.27 eV and 1.53 eV, respectively (see Figures a and b). These results indicate that Li adsorption enhances the electronic density of Zr, with Li atoms acting as electron donors to Zr. The energy gap (Eg) is calculated using the formula Eg = EL − EH. Before Li adsorption, the EH and EL for Zr2C are −3.53 eV and −2.75 eV, respectively. After Li adsorption at site A, these values change to −3.71 eV (EH) and −2.73 eV (EL), respectively, showing a significant alteration in EH and EL due to Li adsorption. The Eg for Zr2C increases from 0.78 eV to 0.98 eV after Li adsorption at site A. A similar behavior is observed at sites B (0.87 eV) and C (0.79 eV), while it remains constant at 0.78 eV at site D.

Fig. 5 (a-e) illustrates similar observations for Zr2CO2. As observed in Fig. 5a, the LUMO PDOS of Zr is significantly higher than that of C and O, indicating that the Zr atom acts as an electron acceptor. The HOMO PDOS for O is higher than that for Zr and C, suggesting that the O atom serves as an electron donor. Additionally, Fig. 5a shows that the O atom has two PDOS peaks, one around −16.33 eV and the other at −11.20 eV. Upon Li adsorption at different sites (A-D), the PDOS peak at −16.33 eV disappears, likely due to the interaction between the Li atom and the O atom, which alters the electronic environment of the O atom and removes the electronic states at that energy level [73,74]. Before Li adsorption, the EH and EL for Zr2CO2 are −4.60 eV and −3.21 eV, respectively. After Li adsorption at site A, these values change to −4.51 eV (EH) and −3.12 eV (EL), respectively, indicating a significant alteration in EH and EL. The Eg for Zr2CO2 increases from 1.38 eV to 1.39 eV after Li adsorption at site A, with the highest Eg values observed at sites C and D (1.59 eV), while the lower values are observed at sites A and B (1.39 eV). Fig. 4f illustrates the PDOS of a Li atom before its adsorption on Zr2C and Zr2CO2. In this figure, the Li atom exhibits three PDOS peaks at 2.72 eV, 0.00 eV, and −2.72 eV. However, after adsorption, as depicted in Fig. 4(b-e) and 5(b-e), the PDOS peaks at 2.72 eV and −2.72 eV disappear, while the peak at 0.00 eV remains, albeit with a reduced area under the curve. This change indicates a significant interaction between the Li atom and both Zr2C and Zr2CO2, confirming that the Li atom acts as an electron donor [73,74]. The reduction in the area under the peak at 0.00 eV suggests a partial transfer of electrons from the Li atom to the substrate, which impacts the electronic properties of the composite material. In Zr2C, after the adsorption of Li at different atomic sites, the projected density of states (PDOS) curves for both Li and Zr atoms show significant changes, as shown in Fig. 4 (a-f). This indicates that the primary interaction in Zr2C is between Li and Zr atoms. Conversely, in Zr2CO2, the PDOS curves for Li and O atoms exhibit noticeable changes post-adsorption, as shown in Fig. 5 (a-e), suggesting that the primary interaction in Zr2CO2 occurs between Li and O atoms. In conclusion, the adsorption of Li atoms significantly impacts the electronic properties of both Zr2C and Zr2CO2, altering their energy levels and gaps. These changes demonstrate the potential of Zr2C and Zr2CO2 for lithium storage applications. The modifications in electronic properties due to Li adsorption enhance the storage capacity and efficiency, making these materials promising candidates for use in lithium-ion batteries and other energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Average open circuit voltage and storage capacity of Zr2C
ce:section.ce:para:
Zr2C MXene was chosen as a representative material to investigate its potential as a lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrode due to its promising structural and electronic properties. Fig. 6 shows the optimized structures of Zr2C with increasing lithium concentrations: Zr2C-3Li, Zr2C-10Li, Zr2C-15Li, and Zr2C-21Li. Since VASP was used to calculate the adsorption energy for a higher number of Li atoms, we compared the adsorption energy for a single Li atom calculated by both methods. We found that the adsorption energy of Zr2C-B-Li obtained from the VASP relaxation step was −2.246 eV, which is slightly less negative than the −2.465 eV reported using G16 in Table 1. Therefore, further relaxation calculations for a higher number of Li atoms will be conducted using VASP due to its lower computational demands. It is worth noting that periodic dipole moments are observed in asymmetric models, particularly those containing an odd number of Li atoms. These moments arise from the inherent asymmetry in charge distribution. While they may influence the absolute values of adsorption energies, the relative trends remain consistent and reliable, as demonstrated by the comparative results in Table 1 and Table 2 . As Li content rises, structural changes are evident, highlighting lithium’s effect on the Zr2C framework and its potential for energy storage applications. The electrochemical performance was evaluated based on the calculated average open circuit voltage (Vave), theoretical specific capacity, and adsorption energies of Li atoms. The calculated Vave, and Ea are written in Table 2. The results indicate that Zr2C shows good lithium storage capability and moderate open circuit voltage characteristics. The Vave decreases from 0.69 V to 0.10 V after the adsorption of various amounts of Li (3Li, 5Li, 10Li, 13Li, 15Li, and 21Li) as shown in Table 2. Initially, the first few Li atoms occupy the most favorable adsorption sites, leading to a higher voltage (0.69 V with 3Li). As more Li atoms are adsorbed, the system becomes increasingly saturated, resulting in a gradual decrease in the open circuit voltage, reaching 0.10 V when the supercell accommodates 21Li atoms. The average open circuit voltage can be calculated using the following equation [75]:
V
ave
E
Zr
2
C
+ x
E
Li
-
E
Zr
2
C L i x
xye
Where EZr2C is the total energy of Zr2C without Li intercalation, ELi is the energy per atom in bulk Li, and EZr2CLix is the total energy of Zr2C after Li intercalation. In addition to the open circuit voltage, the theoretical specific capacity (C) of Zr2C as an electrode for LIBs is calculated to be 2894.47 mA h g−1, which is larger than that of graphite (372 mA h g−1 for LiC6) and Ti3C2O (463 mA h g−1) [75,76]. The higher capacity of Zr2C can be attributed to its ability to accommodate more lithium atoms, enhancing its energy storage capability. The C is calculated using the equation [75]:
C =
nF
Mw × 3.6
Where C is the specific capacity in mA h g−1, n is the number of moles of electrons exchanged, F is the Faraday constant (96,485C/mol), Mw is the molecular weight of Zr2C, and 3.6 is a conversion factor used to convert Coulombs to milliampere-hours. Zr2C’s calculated theoretical specific capacity of 2894.47 mA h g−1 is a significant improvement over graphite’s 372 mA h/g. This enhanced storage capacity is due to Zr2C’s ability to adsorb up to 21Li atoms making it a highly efficient material for LIB electrodes. The increased storage capacity results from the large number of available adsorption sites on both sides of the Zr2C monolayer, which facilitates high lithium loading.

The Ea of Li atoms on the Zr2C MXene surface, as listed in Table 2, exhibit a trend that reflects the interaction strength between Li and the Zr2C monolayer. Initially, the adsorption energy becomes more negative as the number of Li atoms increases, reaching a minimum of 10Li atoms with an adsorption energy of −2.250 eV. This reduction in Ea up to 10Li indicates that the Zr2C monolayer is highly favorable for Li adsorption at this stage, offering strong interactions and stable adsorption sites. After reaching this minimum point at Zr2C-10Li, the Ea begins to increase slightly with further Li adsorption, such as at Zr2C-13Li (−2.249 eV) and Zr2C-21Li (−2.125 eV). This increase in Ea suggests that the surface becomes saturated, and additional Li atoms experience weaker interactions with the Zr2C monolayer. At this stage, the surface adsorption sites are mostly occupied, leading to diminished binding strength for subsequent Li atoms. Although Zr2C MXenes exhibit strong Ea (−2.125 eV), which supports further Li storage, computational limitations restrict extending our analysis to higher Li concentrations.

This study delves into lithium (Li) atom adsorption on Zr2C and Zr2CO2 MXenes, analyzed through density functional theory (DFT), providing in-depth insights into their potential for energy storage applications. The calculations show that Zr2C exhibits superior stability, with a binding energy of −8.09 eV, in contrast to −6.88 eV for Zr2CO2, highlighting Zr2C’s structural resilience and suitability for applications that demand high stability. Both pristine and oxygen-passivated Zr2C demonstrate notable Li adsorption capabilities, with adsorption energies around −3.4 eV, suggesting a strong interaction between Li atoms and the MXene surfaces. Analysis of the electronic structure, including partial density of states (PDOS) and HOMO-LUMO gaps, reveals that oxygen passivation increases Zr2CO2's energy gap from 0.78 eV to 1.38 eV, suggesting enhanced electronic stability. Zr2C’s favorable Li adsorption energies, particularly at surface sites, demonstrate a high affinity for Li, with a maximum specific capacity estimated at 2894.47 mAh g−1 for 21Li atoms. These findings underscore Zr2C’s potential as a promising material for next-generation lithium-ion batteries.
Not applicable.
This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
All authors mentioned in the manuscript have given consent for submission and subsequent publication of the manuscript.

Sakr: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Nahed H. Teleb: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Ghada M. Abdelrazek: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Qinfang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
